title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
Welcome to my first time blogging. Anticipate pure awesomeness without any flaws 🤌.









Above is my fun path of diagnosis. Like many people, I am sure, the first diagnosis was not the first time symptoms appeared. I started having symptoms the moment I popped out of the womb. My intestines decided early on that participating in digestion was just too much work (it probably didn’t help that my food intake ranged from cheese, bread, and occasionally, too many FiberOne bars in a single setting).
I recall once, when I was in middle school, my friend came over to hang out. We got hungry, which was usually every two minutes when you are a young, growing child. I decided to eat some FiberOne bars, and I could down four or five of ’em without any issue. My friend decided to follow in my footsteps, and we found out quickly that perhaps not everyone had a stomach that just won’t process things that fast. She went home and got very sick (whoopsies).
I was also always getting sick, things moving too fast (I know crazy) or not moving enough, and I remember thinking “wow, everyone else is just so much more graceful at handling this than me.” The pediatrician would just shrug his shoulders and chalk it up to a poor diet when my mother brought up my stomach problems. Now, to be fair to the man, my diet wasn’t the best. I had the cravings of a starving 18th-century woman. I wanted carbs and sugar and lots of cheese. So he wasn’t technically wrong, but even when my mom made me eat vegetables and salads and actual food, not microwaved nachos, my stomach was still an absolute disaster.
When my legs started hurting in high school in the middle of the night, to the point of waking me up to stare at the old glow-in-the-dark stars on my ceiling while I waited for it to stop, the doctors didn’t really know what to do. It wasn’t until I was 25, unable to walk faster than the elderly in the grocery store (and boy did I try), that I really pushed to figure things out. I ended up getting diagnosed with non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis (if you ever want to impress anyone at a party, just whip that word out).
I won’t give you too much more of my woes just yet, but I want people to know - you are not alone when you feel like your body is off and doctors can’t see anything. I also want people to know that without advocating for myself and without the doctors who listened to me (giant shout-out to them), I wouldn’t have gotten the diagnoses. And the most important thing about getting diagnosed isn’t to have friends make Pokémon characters for each one to say you’re trying “to catch them all”, but to learn how to treat them and adapt to your body instead of pushing it to work like everyone else’s.

title: My Blog
text:
Hello, I am Melinda! I am an ecologist in training, recovered hater of coding, and full-time caretaker of my immune system. This blog combines all the fun of graduate school in ecological restoration and navigating chronic illness…it is definitely an adventure!
Want to know more? Head to my About page.

title: My Blog
text:
Hello, I am Melinda! I am an ecologist in training, recovered hater of coding, and full-time caretaker of my immune system. This blog combines all the fun of graduate school in ecological restoration and navigating chronic illness…it is definitely an adventure!
Want to know more? Head to my About page.

title: About
text:
Welcome all! I am graduate student at CSU working on my degree of Master of Natural Resource Stewardship Program focusing on ecological restoration and certificate in Data Analysis (a mouthful, I know).
I am also a human trying to manage an angry immune-system (whoot!).
I started this blog to document the intersection of the fun, but sometimes laborious field of ecology and the world of “container maintenance” (a.ka. health issues). I will have it all: exploring the pains and joys of RStudio, hiking up mountains to stare at plants, and the wonders of being a “chronic illness girlie.”

title: Geospatial Coding: Map Assignment
text:
Coding and Mapping, Oh My!
Here is a sample of my blood sweat and tears🩸. I don’t want to shock you, but this is a student sample, emphasis on student. This was a challenging project as much of my experience coding has focused on data analysis without any of the geo-spatial pizzazz. 💥.
I wanted to look at railroads, eco-regions, and wildlife hot-spots around railroads in Colorado for this assignment I did for ESS523A.
This is a setup script I was taught to have and use throughout my projects. It is super helpful because then you don’t have to continually tell R to “please load these eight billion packages I need by name”. Just put the setup into a Rscript and save it as setup.R and off you go!


Set up script
function to check for package installation, then install and load libraries.

packageLoad <-
  function(x) {
    for (i in 1:length(x)) {
      if (!x[i] %in% installed.packages()) {
        install.packages(x[i])
      }
      library(x[i], character.only = TRUE)
    }
  }

vector of packages to load

packages <- c('tidyverse',
              'palmerpenguins',
              'sf',
              'terra',
              'tmap',
              'rmarkdown',
              'tigris',
              'elevatr',
              'rgbif',
              'soilDB',
              'plotly',
              'mapview',
              "leaflet")

packageLoad(packages)

First I actually needed to get data for roads (Vector Data).

source("setup.R")
rails <- rails()
CO_boundary <- states() %>% filter(STUSPS == "CO")
CO_rails <- st_filter(rails, CO_boundary)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = CO_rails)

Next I needed to get data for Colorado Ecoregions (Vector Data).

CO_ecoregions <- st_read("C:/Users/melin/Geospatial/co_eco_l3/co_eco_l3.shp")
#Map came out tilted so need to transform to different CSR
CO_ecoregions_prj <- st_transform(CO_ecoregions, st_crs(CO_rails))

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = CO_ecoregions_prj, aes(fill = US_L3NAME)) +
  geom_sf(data = CO_rails) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Colorado Ecoregions Map")

And then I needed to obtain data for migratory animals (Mule Deer, Elk, Black Bear).

#make a string of species names to use in the 'occ_data' function
species <- c("Cervus canadensis", "Odocoileus hemionus", "Ursus americanus")

#also make a string of common names
common_name <- c("Elk", "Mule Deer", "Black Bear")

#map2
species_occ <-function(sci_name, comm_name){
t_occ <-
    occ_data(
      scientificName = sci_name,
      hasCoordinate = TRUE,
      geometry = st_bbox(CO_ecoregions_prj),
      limit = 2000 
    ) %>%
    .$data 
  
  t_occ$ID <- comm_name
  
  t_occ <-
    t_occ %>% distinct(decimalLatitude, decimalLongitude, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
    dplyr::select(Species = ID,
                  decimalLatitude,
                  decimalLongitude,
                  year,
                  month,
                  basisOfRecord) 
return(t_occ)
}

output_species <- purrr::map2(species, common_name, species_occ)

occ <- bind_rows(output_species) %>% 
  st_as_sf(coords = c("decimalLongitude", "decimalLatitude"), crs = 4236)

occ_prj <- st_transform(occ, st_crs(CO_ecoregions_prj))

Next, compile the data.

#make data more manageable, group by ecoregions and distance to railroad
railroad_buffer <- st_buffer(CO_rails, dist = 500)
occ_prj$railroad_intersections <- st_intersects(occ_prj, railroad_buffer)


#join data
eco_rail_occ <- st_join(occ_prj, CO_ecoregions_prj) %>%
  mutate(near_rails_500m = if_else(lengths(railroad_intersections) == 0, FALSE, TRUE))

#summarize
all_sum <- eco_rail_occ %>% 
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(US_L3NAME) %>% 
  summarise(total_occ = n(), 
            total_railroad = sum(near_rails_500m == TRUE),
            percent_railroad = (sum(near_rails_500m == TRUE)/total_occ)*100, .groups = "drop") %>%
  filter(!is.na(US_L3NAME))
class(all_sum)

eco_spatial <- CO_ecoregions_prj %>%
  inner_join(all_sum, by = "US_L3NAME")



Map Data

tmap_mode("plot")

map <-tm_shape(CO_ecoregions_prj) +
  tm_polygons(fill = "US_L3NAME", fill.scale = tm_scale_categorical(values = "brewer.pastel1"),
              fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Ecoregions III")) +
tm_shape(eco_spatial) +
  tm_bubbles(size = "percent_railroad",
             fill = "blue",
             size.legend = tm_legend(title = " %Wildlife Near Railroad", orientation = "landscape")) +
tm_shape(CO_rails) +
  tm_lines(col = "red") +
  tm_add_legend(labels = "Railroads", type = "lines", col = "black", title = "Infrastructure") +
tm_title(
  "Combined Wildlife Proximity to Railroads in Colorado\n(Elk, Mule Deer, Black Bear)", size = 1.5, fontface = "bold", position = tm_pos_out("center", "top")) +
  tm_layout(frame = FALSE, 
            legend.outside = TRUE,
            legend.outside.position = "right",
            legend.outside.size = 0.3,
            legend.text.size = .6,
            legend.title.size = .8) +
  tm_scalebar(position = c("left", "bottom")) +
  tm_compass(position = c("right", "top")) +
  tm_credits("Map by Melinda Stueber | Data Sources: EPA, TIGRIS, GBIF", position = c("left", "bottom"), size = 0.5, color = "black") 

map
tmap_save(map, "C:/Users/melin/Geospatial/Rails_Wildlife_CO_map.png")




Colorado Wildlife and Railroad Map


It isn’t the prettiest map in the world, but she’s mine and I was proud that it actually generated something. So if you’ve learned anything, know that if I can code, you can too! We are all learning, it is part of the fun, sometimes anger-inducing, process of R.
